region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Rice congee with century egg, lean pork, spring onion, ginger, sesame oil

ontology.foodon_label: rice porridge (congee) with meat and egg
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has rice porridge/congee as a category but does not have a specific entry for century egg and pork congee. Closest parent is rice porridge (congee) with meat and egg toppings.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice congee with century egg, diced lean pork loin, spring onion, ginger, and sesame oil
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A003J is the closest FoodEx2 code for rice-based porridge/congee. No specific code exists for century egg and pork congee variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not typically encode composite prepared foods at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of century egg and pork congee as served at Singapore kopitiams and hawker centres is approximately 400g.

nutrient_source.title: Century Egg and Pork Congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥) — Health Promotion Board Singapore / Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Century Egg and Pork Congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥). HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Values are accepted as verified.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Congee and Analogous Porridge Foods — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain rice congee has a published GI of approximately 86 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Century egg and pork congee uses the same highly gelatinized rice base. The addition of lean pork protein moderates the GI slightly, yielding an estimated GI of 82 for this composite dish. GL per 400g serving calculated as: (82 × 38g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 31.2. This is consistent with the input record's referenced assessment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from century egg (pidan) and soy seasoning — 285mg sodium per 100g, delivering approximately 1140mg per standard 400g bowl
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit century egg and pork congee to a small portion (200g, ~570mg sodium) and request no additional soy sauce; consider replacing the century egg with a plain egg to substantially reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 82) and high glycemic load per serving (GL 31.2) from the highly gelatinized rice congee base, which is rapidly absorbed
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to a small bowl (200g, GL ~15.6), pair with additional protein-rich foods, and be aware that a standard 400g bowl delivers approximately 38g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate equivalent to 1.5 cups of steamed rice in glycaemic terms.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (285mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (62mg/100g) from century egg; however, saturated fat is low (0.5g/100g) and the dish is dairy-free with lean pork as the protein source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium load per serving (1140mg per 400g bowl) and moderate cholesterol contribution from century egg; limiting portion size to 200g and omitting additional seasoning is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), good selenium (10mcg/100g) and zinc (0.8mg/100g) from lean pork, and B12 from century egg support moderate anti-inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Century egg and pork congee is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its low saturated fat content and provision of selenium and zinc; sodium should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Century Egg and Pork Congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥, Pídàn shòuròu zhōu; Cantonese: Pei Daan Sau Yuk Juk) is the most popular congee variant served at Singapore kopitiams and hawker centre congee stalls. It is a Cantonese-origin dish consisting of a silky, creamy rice porridge base made by simmering white rice in a large volume of water or stock for several hours until the grains fully break down and gelatinize. The porridge is topped with sliced century egg (pidan — a duck egg preserved in an alkaline mixture of clay, ash, salt, and quicklime for several weeks, resulting in a dark translucent jelly white and a dark grey-green creamy yolk with an intense, ammonia-forward flavour) and tender slivers of lean pork loin that are added raw to the hot congee and cook through in minutes. The dish is finished with fried shallots, julienned ginger, spring onion rings, a few drops of sesame oil, and white pepper. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 72 kcal, 5.2g protein, 1.8g fat, 9.5g carbohydrate, and 285mg sodium. The century egg contributes significant sodium, cholesterol (62mg/100g), vitamin B12, and selenium. Clinically, the dish has a very high glycemic index (GI 82) due to the highly gelatinized starch of the congee base, making it a high-GL food (GL 31.2 per 400g bowl). It is a quintessential comfort food and convalescent dish, widely consumed for breakfast, as a late-night supper, and during illness. It is dairy-free and gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the plain congee analogue (GI ~86) with a defensible downward adjustment for protein content, consistent with published methodology. All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and plausible.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0g per 100g as reported by HPB — confirmed zero by source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1. Water content is not reported by HPB and is set to -1. Copper (0.04mg/100g), manganese (0.08mg/100g), and vitamin K (1.5mcg/100g) are from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 and should be treated as estimated values. GI of 82 is an analogous estimate based on plain rice congee (GI ~86, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted for lean pork protein contribution. GL per serving calculated on 400g default portion: (82 × 9.5g carbohydrate per 100g × 4) / 100 = 31.2.
